summary: I came to the United States when I was twenty-one years old, in 1914, to join my husband Seno who had returned earlier. We lived in Florin, California, where we farmed grapes and strawberries until the war broke out. Life here was very hard, especially during the Great Depression in 1933 when we struggled to make ends meet. Despite the difficulties, I found support from Christian friends and the Salvation Army, which helped me through tough times.

I was born and raised in Japan, where I attended elementary school until I was twelve. My father left Japan for the United States in May after I graduated, but he died shortly after from a railroad accident. I stayed in Japan alone for three years after marrying Seno, who had gone to the U.S. before me. I finally joined him in the U.S. despite not wanting to leave Japan, especially because my mother was running a weaving business at home.

When I arrived in the United States, I was processed at Angel Island where we had to undergo a physical examination and answer many questions. We stayed there for three or four days before my husband picked me up. It was a difficult but necessary step in entering the country, and I remember the uncertainty during those days.

My husband and I settled in Florin, California, where we farmed grapes and strawberries on a 10-acre farm. We later bought a 15-acre farm to expand, but strawberries didn’t grow well there and it took years to succeed with grapes. Life was hard, especially during the Great Depression, and eventually we had to sell the farm before the war forced us to move to an assembly center.

During the hardest times of the Great Depression, a kind man named Mr. Yamada from Sacramento helped us by giving rice and food so we could survive. I still visit his grave to thank him for his kindness. Sacramento was also where some of our church friends lived, and it was a place connected to the support network that helped us through difficult times.

A friend who was a member of the Salvation Army introduced me to Reverend Baba from Oxnard, who gave a sermon that deeply moved me. After receiving his blessing, I felt a heavy sensation in my head and cried for days, but eventually I found happiness and strength through the Salvation Army meetings. Their support was a turning point in my spiritual life during very difficult times.

Reverend Baba from Oxnard played an important role in my spiritual journey. A friend took me to hear his sermon, and after his blessing, I experienced a profound emotional and spiritual change. Oxnard became significant to me because of the Salvation Army’s influence through Reverend Baba’s ministry.

When the war broke out, we were evacuated to the Manzanar assembly center. Life there was rough but we lived in relative comfort compared to what we had endured before. I worked as a kitchen helper and attended church regularly, which was a source of comfort during those difficult times.
The transcript does not mention Denver or any experiences related to it, so I have no memories to share about that place.
There is no mention of Washington, D.C. in my story, so I do not have any experiences or memories connected to that place.

I remember hearing about the attack on Pearl Harbor and feeling worried about Japan. My husband said Japan had done a terrible thing, and we were afraid of what might happen to us afterward. The attack marked a turning point in our lives, leading to our evacuation and the loss of our farm.

The hardest time in my life was during the Great Depression, especially in 1933 when we struggled to sell our grapes and strawberries. We barely made enough to survive and were helped by kind neighbors like Mr. Yamada from Sacramento who gave us rice and food. Despite the poverty and hunger, none of my children got sick, and I found strength through my Christian faith and community.
